text:
VALORANT has become stale. After 6 years of gameplay the way you play maps in higher elo has become highly optimized. Before I knew how to play haven for example I used to be so much fun, trying out new things to outplay the enemy (I was also 2000 something elo below where I am now). Now it is just hold deep lines for info and stack the correct bomb sites. This invariance in gamplay has become boring and new agent releases don't change the way you play different maps either.

After playing VALORANT less actively I discovered TFT and what keeps me queuing is the high variance. Your augments and shops change every game introducing variance to each one. Sure this also makes some lowroll games unplayable but others incredibly fun where the stars align for you. And TFT keeps being fresh every two weeks where there are balance changes and a new set is introduced every couple months.

Now VALORANT can't change its fundamental gameplay mechanics and gimmicks every couple months the way TFT does but they could introduce variance between games. Make shields cost less money on pistol round, have random cheap guns make more money, or special effects on lower win rate champions. Bans would introduce variance but rather restrict gamplay than encourage alternative gamplay.

With Icebox supposedly coming back, it will in all likelihood just be viper, sova, killjoy plus two idiots locking reyna and jett. And if you change the change the core of those three agents you put yourself at a disadvantage. I am just tired of playing the same game for 6 years. idk how you guys feel about this but lately there has not been much of an incentive for me to play. Gun buddies and battlepasses just don't do it for me anymore
label: insight

text:
Valorant has access to enormous amounts of telemetry data—scans, utility value, space creation, information gained, damage prevented, and trade setups—yet much of the visible performance evaluation still appears heavily correlated with kills. This encourages selfish play patterns and undervalues many of the actions that actually win rounds.I do not know exactly what goes into ACS and rr gain/ loss but I do know a large part of it comes down to K/D and clutches. However, that is such a bad representation of your value to the match. Here are the following most glaring issues that has led this misrepresentaion to inflating the rr of undeserving players and suppressing good support agents.

1) Putting too much value on K/D has led to so many players just playing for kills and exits that it's a problem. This can be seen in Diamond- Ascendant (and even low immortal) where we often get duelists who just play for kills and have no regard of risking their lives in crucial moments of the match. conversely, it has led to selfless duelists who hard entry and often end up with bad kds both gain less and lose more rr than they deserve. In my opinion, if someone is on the duelist role and they die first, it should have little to no regard on how it contributes ( or takes away from) the ACS. For support agents the acs calculation should stay the same but due to the nature of the duelist role, KD shouldn't be a main determining factor of ACS.

Conversely, exit frags should contribute little to no contribution to the final ACS. Everyone in every rank has had people who give up on the game and just play for exits to minimise the rr they lose. that actually works and ideally shouldn't. If that strategy is effective, the system is rewarding the wrong behavior.

2) Assists from support agents should contribute more to the final ACS and rr. I've had so many games on sova with 15+ assists and a large margin win to only get +19 because of less kills. In my opinion, Initiator scans that lead to kills, smoke assists, flash assists, kayo suppression, vulnerables etc should all lead to a greater contribution to the ACS and rr especially when they come at crucial moments of the game like post plants and retakes.

3) Utility usage that does not lead to an assist but nonetheless contribute to massive impact must be evaluated. Some examples include:

Sova drone clears an entire site but nobody gets a kill.

Omen smoke denies an Operator angle for 20 seconds.

Viper wall forces a rotation.

Cypher cam spots a lurk.

KAY/O knife reveals four players.

These can completely win rounds without generating assists.

4) Ultimately, there should be role-specific evaluations. the ideal playstyle of each role differs so much that judging them all under the same framework is stupid and does not work for a tactical shooter with agent variance. Different roles create value in different ways, and the performance system should reflect that.


Upvote
14

Downvote

9
Go to comments


Share
label: insight

text: The Valiant Hero bundle, except for the slightly atrocious Ares skin, Is a VERY underrated bundle imo. The Vandal is a masterpiece of Riot design. It gives that 2021 Valorant aesthetic. The handpainted textures on this Vandal are astonishingly detailed, with grey fades around the curves of the model to emphasize wear and tear and add to the soft, cloudy aesthetic the rest of the skin has. The smoke momentarily pauses their flow as you fire your bullets and the mountains on the skin rise up as you pull out the gun and as you reload. When you take the magazine out, the details on the side of the gun disappears and reappears when you reinsert the magazine with its own rising animation, a very unique design among Valorant skins. It has its own custom reload animation thats basically the opposite if the Reaver reload. Reaver uses magic to move the mag into the chamber, then you use your hand to cock the gun. Valiant Hero uses normal movement to insert the mag, then uses magic to cock the gun. Thats something many exclusive or ultra bundles cant do. Personally, and I know this is a VERY hot take, It’s design is better than Kuronami’s. Kuronami’s design is simply flat geometric pieces of interwoven katana-like pieces and whatever the hell the gun is made of. It looks cool, but there’s not much going on except for the fact it looks cool. Sure, a really good reload, but thats basically it. Ok im done with my 10pm rant about how i would absolutely cop valiant hero if appeared in the night market kBai.

text:
The purpose of this post is to help people who may be grinding out or spending time on aim trainers before they have become very good or have mastered the fundamental mechanics of the game. Hopefully this may help some of you see faster improvement in your own games and ranked journeys.

A lot of players spend far too much time playing aim trainers thinking it will make a significant impact to their performance and improvement in Valorant. This post isn't to say aim trainers are "bad", but there are much more efficient warm ups, drills, and forms of practice you could be doing that will cause you to see visible improvements in-game a lot faster than grinding out aim trainers will. This also isn't to say you won't see ANY improvement in your mechanics from using aim trainers, but just that the improvement will be slower and in smaller amounts compared to the improvement you would see if you mastered the fundamentals.

When you're below Immortal (and even many players who are still in low-mid immortal) you still haven't mastered the basic fundamentals of the game, so you should be drilling and focusing on these before adding aim trainers to your warmup routine. Once you have a very good grasp on the fundamentals, this is when you want to start thinking about adding aim trainers to your routine.

Most good players use them as a warmup rather than for actual improvement. Pretty much all of your kills in val will come from a combination of your movement and crosshair placement. Raw aim does matter, but significantly less than every other mechanic related to aim/gunfights.

A much more efficient approach to improvement would be to master the more crucial mechanics in-game, and once you have a very good grasp on them you THEN start adding aim trainers for that extra buffer.
label: insight

text:
I know I'm part of the choir of the thousands of people complaining about this but out of all the time I've played this game, I've never seen an MMR system this bad in any game, and I've played League during the time where role-specific ranks were added.

I am a Diamond player. I've been Diamond ever since Ep5. While I will concede that I might just be bad at the game if I can't climb past Diamond, but this new MMR system has made that possibility all but impossible. In my past few games, I have been queued into high gold low plat lobbies as one of 2 sole D1s. Riot's new system basically forces those Diamond players to hard carry their games to climb.

My second most recent game, I was playing KJ on Ascent. I lost 7/13 going 22/16/4. Can't say I haven't tried, but I was paired against 2 P1s, 2 G2, and one D1. I had 1 P2, 2 G3s, and a G1. I lost 20 RR for that game.

My most recent game as KJ again, we won 13/6 going 12/9/3. I held my team, I played my role, even though i wasn't top frag, the rest of my team did well. Roughly same rank dispersal, and I only gained 13 RR.

Like, tell me why I'm punished for being queued with lower rank players, not being able to carry or perform like Chad Smith on the drums, and gain less and lose more for things that are well outside my control? I can't control who I'm paired with, I can't control how well my team will perform, I can only control how I perform, and in this system, if I don't perform like fucking Superman, I get punished.

Just pair me with high plats and low diamonds. Pair me with the actual rank I SHOULD be competing with, not those who I am performing above par with. All of this didn't even mention the argument of paring higher ranked players with lower ranked ones creates, which is the inability to actually learn and get better. I'm not going to get better playing against golds and plats. I will get better playing with higher skilled opponents, even if they kick my ass.

The old MMR system was fine. It truly was just fine. It was rough around the edges, sure, but it worked consistently.

text:
With the state of the current ranked ladder, Valorant would significantly benefit from separating the ranked queues that allow for stacking and solo/duo as their own ladders (just how riot also does in League of Legends).

A ranked ladder that allows for solo/duo queue max gives a much better and more accurate representation of individual-skill than one that allows for 3 stacking, 5 stacking etc (especially when there are no rank limits to who you can actually 5stack with).

The stacking allows people to be placed in ranks they don't belong in a lot easier. For example if someone were naturally a silver player and got carried to plat by stacking with diamonds, ascendants, immortals etc. Their visible rank would show as plat, and then if that person decides to queue solo one day they're going to be in plat lobbies ruining the game for real plat players, despite only being a silver-level player themselves.

Separating ranked ladders and having a solo/duo limit would also help decrease smurfing and boosting would be less prevalent than it currently is.

Does anyone know if Riot has addressed why they don't have a solo/duo ladder and a flex ladder the same way they do in League?

text:
I'm actually shocked at how much more difficult the game has become simply from Gold 3 to Platinum 1. Now I know what you're thinking, "you're just bad"... and you're probably right.

After getting promoted to Plat, I began to notice how much better my opponents were to the point it started to feel a little weird. But before I took to Reddit, I played out about 10 games to see if there were any patterns, and here are my findings, and I promise you there are no exaggerations:

There are no longer gunfights

In Gold, I'd find myself often in some fun gun fights. Peeking and trading shots at each other for a few seconds before someone got the kill. In Plat, I'm finding nearly 80% of my deaths are from a Ferrari Peek 1 tap. Oftentimes, I get domed before I even know what I'm looking at. The level of aim mechanics players have now is unfathomable. I sometimes wonder what these people are eating for breakfast.

No one comms

This was the most noticeable. In Gold, I was getting teamed up with so many talkative people, having a good time. Sure, there were the odd toxic or annoying person, but honestly, about 75% of my games had a full team of people communicating. Out of the 10 games of Plat lobbies I played, I may have heard 2 voices. It's truly a bizarre phenomenon.

The lobbies are severely 1-sided

If you look at my career, I was chock-full of very close games, win or lose. Many, many games that go 20+ rounds, and those lobbies are full of even players, with everyone hitting double-digit kills, etc. Plat has been a completely different story. I thought Riot fixed the balancing issue, but I suppose they forgot to check on Plat lobbies. Out of the 10 games I played, we won 2 of them, and every single game, and I mean every single game, was a one-sided bloodbath, typically with 1 or 2 players going 20+ kills.

Overall, I'm really not having a good time in Platinum. Finally reaching the rank gave me a big feeling of accomplishment, and that feeling was quickly kicked in the nuts. Platinum is the least fun I've ever had playing ranked. I want to go back to Gold, where people are actually fun to play with.

It really is bizarre, the difference in gameplay I'm having. It's not like we're getting tactically outplayed; we are simply just getting run down and 1-tapped constantly. text:
I spent hundreds of hours improving, learning the game, supporting teammates, keeping morale up, and grinding my way to Ascendant 1. Getting there felt rewarding.


The problem started after I reached that rank.


Since then, a significant portion of my matches seem to be decided by factors completely outside my control: AFKs, trolls, smurfs, and accounts that appear to be boosted or prepared for resale. I've dropped to the point where seeing Platinum 3 feels more common than maintaining my previous rank.


Some games feel unwinnable from the start. There are matches where three teammates finish with fewer than 8 kills each, leaving one or two players to carry. If we manage to win, great. If not, it's often a heavy RR loss.


What frustrates me even more is how cheating cases are handled. Losing RR because of a cheater feels bad enough, but when that player is eventually banned, the compensation often feels insignificant compared to the points that were lost.


I'm not posting this to complain about a single bad match. I'm genuinely curious:


Do other players feel that the current ranked experience is too heavily influenced by trolls, AFKs, smurfs, and cheaters? If so, what changes would you like Riot to make to improve competitive integrity?

text:
The reason I am posting this, is to share a mindset tip that I have used to help others in their ranked journey that may also benefit some people here on this subreddit. After having coached thousands of players I have noticed a very prevalent pattern and error that people make in their mental-game and approach to Valorant ranked. People often have the tendency to focus on and hyper fixate on the RESULT of their comp games rather than having a performance-based mindset. This is also something that happens particularly in games where they may perform well but still lost (for whatever the reason may be, even if they had horrendous teammates, etc). Focusing on these sort of games won't do anything positive for you and will just lead to a negative mindset and possibly tilt.

Something I like to tell my students all the time is that you can "win" even when you lose. What I mean by that is, when you lose a game you basically have 2 options in your mind. You can either just accept the loss and move on without thinking about it (only focusing on the result of the game) or, you can switch your mindset to focus on your own gameplay rather than the result of the match itself. Your "win" is that even if you technically lost the match, you still gained something from it that you can immediately implement in your next game and increase your chances of winning your next game/s because you have IMMEDIATELY noticed something that you can improve upon right away. This leads to consistent fast-tracked improvement.

Focus 100% on your own performance ALWAYS, don't go into ranked games with your main focus before you queue up being "I want to win X amount of games, win X amount of RR, get to this rank by X amount of time, etc". This way of thinking leads many people to approach ranked with a sort of "gambling" mindset. When you focus too much on the OUTCOME of the game itself, people often find themselves queueing up again for the sole purpose of trying to gain their RR back or just getting the win back etc. You have to approach comp with the mindset that your rank will simply be the result of your individual performance over time.

By switching your mindset every time you queue and whenever you're in a match, to focus entirely on your performance, any mistakes you make as they happen, things you could've done better, just analyzing your gameplay as a whole, that allows you to identify areas of your own game to work on which you can then implement in your future games much faster. By implementing them in your future games, you will perform better consistently because you are actively improving and will inevitably rank up.

You should try to remember that your rank is simply a byproduct of your performance over time. Don't think about whether you're winning a game, losing a game, how much RR you're winning or losing etc. Focus fully on your performance and let the results just be a byproduct of that. And remember that even if you lose, you can still take your "wins" from it.
label: insight

text:
The numbers don't lie: Gekko consistently sits among the lowest-picked agents in high-ranked play, especially in Immortal and above. Despite multiple balance adjustments, his presence in the meta remains almost nonexistent, while initiators like Fade and Sova continue to dominate because of the reliability and value they provide to their teams. When an agent remains one of the least selected picks after receiving buffs, it is a clear sign that the core issues have not been addressed.
The reality is simple: Gekko's utility is too inconsistent, too risky to recover, and often rewards the player less than other initiators for the same investment. In many situations, choosing Gekko means voluntarily putting yourself at a disadvantage compared to other options.

I believe Gekko needs another buff, because the last one proved that very little has changed.

At high competitive levels, Dizzy becomes unreliable when used alone and usually requires combinations with other utility to generate consistent value. At lower levels, Gekko's kit feels clunky due to the time required to recover abilities and reposition effectively, especially in post-plant situations.

Another major issue is Dizzy itself. The debuff it applies is arguably the least impactful vision impairment among initiator abilities. Unlike many other flashes and blinds in the game, affected players largely maintain their spatial awareness. On top of that, its duration is same to Phoenix's flash, despite Phoenix's flash being significantly faster, more efficient, and harder to react to (all of this and in 2x).

The ability recovery mechanic is also far less rewarding than it appears on paper. In many situations, taking the risk to retrieve an orb is simply not worth it. Players gain more value by using that time to reposition, take space, or prepare for the next engagement. When one of an agent's defining mechanics is often ignored because it is not worth the risk, that should be a warning sign.

To finally make Gekko a viable and competitive initiator, I propose the following changes:

Dizzy blind duration increased from 2.0s to 2.2s (+0.2s to max potency).

Ability deployment time reduced from 1.5s to 1s.

Ability recovery cooldown reduced by 15%.

Mosh-Pit DoT damage per tick increased from 1 to 2 (added via suggestions in comments)

These changes would strengthen Gekko's identity without making him overpowered. They would reward active utility usage, make ability recovery a meaningful mechanic, and allow him to compete more effectively with the initiators that currently dominate the meta.

Gekko should not become the best initiator in Valorant. He should simply become a viable choice that players can pick without being told they're throwing the game.
label: insight

text:
Hey everyone! I've never been much of a competitive games player in general, and when I did play them, it was always super casually. I quit Valorant a while back because it felt like it took so much effort to play consistently. Rust built up quick (which to be fair might have been a major skill issue) and after every couple of days not playing I felt like I suddenly lost the ability to shoot people and play the game like anything other than a bumbling buffoon. It was very quickly turning into just getting shot instantly the moment I made contact with any enemy, which again I don't blame the game for, I know it's totally on me.

However, recently I've been actually improving a lot in another game I played in a similar way, League of Legends. (bear with me I'm mentioning this for a reason) The way I did all of this imporiving isn't exactly practice, I played enough already for that, but rather just gathering game knowledge. This is a really good motivator for me, because I get super absorbed into reading/watching material about a game I'm into. I know probably doing stuff like aimlabs and such is a great way to improve, but I find it really hard to motivate myself to do that unless I'm deeply invested in the game. However, for me gathering knowledge is something I very quickly get absorbed in, and I'm actually able to motivate myself through it very easily.

Having said that, I want to actually see if I can become more consistent in Valorant, but I need some reading/watching material to hook me in. It can be anything remotely related to game knowledge. General guides, map intricacies, agent mastery videos, fundamentals etc. What matters is that I can move a bit towards feeling comfortable in the game. I don't know if this is just a thing for me specifically or if it's a general shared experience, but I feel a lot more comfortable in a game when I understand it, and being comfortable is usually how I consistently grow.

Of course the question is, do you guys have any recommendations for me?

EDIT: Yes I understand that the best way to improve at a game is by playing the game. I'm not asking about that, I'm specifically asking where I could find supplementary game knowledge so I can hype myself up about Valorant whenever I'm not able to actually play but have some time for myself

text:
One thing Valorant players need to understand is that not everyone experiences tilt the same way.

Some players can get criticized, bottom-frag, lose five games in a row, and move on because their chemical makeup allows them to.

OTHERS CAN’T!!

When I’m playing well, I don’t care what anyone says. When I’m having a bad game, I become emotionally reactive and it gets super hard to focus on anything BUT tilting, because I want my emotions acknowledged and validated.

For years I’ve listened to Valorant and CS players saying “just fix your mental” as if it’s just a switch you can turn ofd. What I’ve found is that my ability to learn and improve is directly tied to my negative life experiences (and the uncontrollable mental consequences they’ve had on me).

Once I started addressing the things that were stressing me outside the game (high cortisol), having tilt became a complete non-issue. I’m calm, self-aware, and willing to look at my mistakes objectively instead of getting stuck in my emotions, because the cortisol isn’t pushing voices into my head.

That’s why I think a lot of advice about tilt misses the point, and is the fault of players that have no empathy. The issue isn’t Valorant itself, but the fact that a competitive environment where communication is key exposes some people’s mental issues.

If you’re struggling with tilt, don’t beat yourself up over it. Figure out what’s actually causing the stress, because fixing that will probably do more for your rank than any aim routine or VOD review ever could.
label: insight

text:
Just finished a Valorant match with 4 friends, and honestly, I'm still trying to process what happened.

This wasn't one of those situations where you're wondering if someone has wallhacks or a subtle aimbot. If it had been that, we'd probably still be debating it and looking for evidence in a replay.

Nop..

From the very first round, the guy was blatantly cheating.

He got 5 headshots while literally jumping around and spinning. Not trying to hide it. Not pretending to be legit. Just full-on spinbotting across the map and instantly deleting everyone he saw.

Then he proceeded to do the same thing for the next 11 rounds.... Twelve rounds in total.

Twelve consecutive rounds of aces, headshots, spinning, jumping, and generally making a mockery of the match.

Eventually Vanguard woke up, the red screen appeared, the player got banned, and the match was terminated.

Which brings me to the part I find both hilarious and concerning.

We're constantly hearing about Vanguard, kernel-level anti-cheat, new detection methods, improvements, updates, and how advanced the system is.

Yet a guy who looked like he had downloaded the most obvious cheat imaginable was allowed to run around spinbotting in plain sight for 12 rounds before the game finally decided something might be wrong.

And because the match was terminated, we can't even watch the replay/demo afterwards. The entire thing basically disappeared.

So my genuine question is:
What is going on?

I'm not complaining that he wasn't banned eventually. He was.

I'm wondering how a player can spend half a match spinbotting as blatantly as humanly possible before the anti-cheat intervenes.

Has anyone else run into something like this recently, or did we just witness the rarest Vanguard success story ever?
label: rant

text:
Aftershock (damage burst) and Faultline (concussion) swap places with some minor differences, making Aftershock Breach's new signature ability. Additionally, Flashpoint gains an option to be charged.

[C] Faultline (Aftershock): FIRE the charge to set a concussion blast through the wall. The concussion has a 6m, 180-degree cone (semicircle) radius with a 0.8s windup. How you deploy it is similar to the current Aftershock in game.

[Q] Flashpoint: Gain an option to charge the ability by HOLDING FIRE, which creates an audible cue (at Breach's location) within a 35m radius. Charging this ability shaves off some of the flash’s windup duration. It will automatically fire once it reaches the max charge and it can't be cancelled.

[E] Aftershock (Faultline): HOLD FIRE to increase the distance. RELEASE to set a slow-acting dual burst. It will have a maximum distance of 30m with a width of 5m (50s cooldown). How you deploy it is similar to the current Faultline in game.

This swap change helps Breach become more effective at clearing and weakening multiple key positions or angles for his teammates while scaling. The reason for the swap is simply that, in my opinion, the concussion effect is inconsistent, ineffective, and unreliable.

The flash change adds skill expression through good positioning, map knowledge, and game sense (gauging enemies' positions). It then rewards players by making the flash harder to dodge (similar to Skye's flash). The maximum amount of windup duration that can be shaved off is open to interpretation.
label: insight

I'm a shotgun only player with 2k hours / 40,000 judge kills over 3 years or so. I've played through many changes to Shotguns.

I currently think they are in a terribly weird place atm...

The Judge/Shorty almost have no effective range. They should be powerful close range (just as sniper rifles are good at long range).

Imagine if Riot nerfed sniper rifles because 'They were too effective at long range' etc.

I think the best way to nerf Shotguns (if they even needed it) is to keep the damage/accuracy exactly how they were before nerfs - just increase the price.

The main reason I do well playing Shotguns is because they perform too well for their price point - I can buy a Judge or Bucky every round, even after losing multiple in a row.

Nerf the price, now I gotta save. Fewer Shotguns per round = less player frustration.

Riot out there nerfing everything until valorant feels like a pillow fight.

What are your thoughts on it? Are Shotguns currently as lethal as they should be or would there be a better way to balance them?

text:
Obviously, there's a lot of content creators that think Valorant is on the decline. Some for clickbait, some for genuine beliefs. I want to find the community's answer on this (if it gains traction). I would like to state that I personally believe that Valorant is not dying. It is arguably the most popular FPS game, debated with CS2. What I want to know is if it's having a LINEAR declining playerbase. What do I mean by this?

(The following statement uses data from SteamDB, which may contain inacurracies)

Games, in most cases, have good times and bad times. Take, for example, Overwatch and Overwatch 2 (yes, I'm separating them differently). Overwatch had good times, like pre-Briggette release, recently, and the start of Overwatch 2, and some bad times, like the middle of Overwatch 2, the GOATS meta, Briggette. Something with a Linear Decline in playerbase is, for example, Rocket League. Excluding the Jynxzi effect and console player counts, Rocket League has been on a steady and decline since 2022 with no signs of reaching their former high. Rocket League still has a good playerbase, but it's still a decline nevertheless.

Is Valorant in the state of good and bad or, looking at the data and trends, in a linear decline? If it's the latter, though I doubt it, why is it not happening to other competetitive games? Is it lack of change (Counter proof: CS2)? Is it too much change (Counter proof: Apex)? Is there too much going on (Counter proof: League)?

I just want a clear answer on this. I don't want to see Valorant die because I have a competetive spark with an analytical brain. Forgive me for getting facts wrong or saying the wrong things. At the end of the day, I'm still just a curious person.

Sub-question but why do people say the Valorant community is the worst? Every community in every competetive game is horrible in their own way, so what makes Valorant different?
label: insight

text:
Passive: Soul Orb. Now only needs damage to grant a charge. Just like Clove.

Signature: Leer. Deploys like the normal Reyna Leer, but is an activation based ability. It only has one charge. You can place it in midair or halfway inside walls. Turns invisible 0.5 seconds after deployment. Reveals in a 3m radius. Costs 250 credits and recharges after 60 seconds. Has 1 HP.

Ability 2: Spectral Glimpse. Deploys like Leer, but with a maximum range of 10 meters. It tags all enemies within its line of sight (360 degree) with a red question mark on the minimap. Is invincible, takes 0.25 seconds to deploy, lasts 0.3 seconds. Costs 200 credits and 1 soul orb.

Ability 3: Wandering Souls. Sends forth a Paranoia-sized wave of tangled shadows. Marks all enemy utility on the minimap for 6 seconds and makes any invisible utility visible with cute lil shadow orbs trailing around invisible utility. It concusses any enemy hit for 1.5 seconds. Cost: 350 credits.

Ultimate: Guide of Souls. Sends forth a pack of big (cute) slithering shadow orbs that travel underground. Whilst underground, travels at about 70% of Skye cabbage speed. Reveals a slight dark wispy trail when in 12m of an enemy and jumps aboveground when at 5m And accelerates to a bit faster than walking speed. When underground, has 150hp. When aboveground, has 100 hp. Damage doesnt carry over. For example, if the orb sustains any damage while underground, Itll heal to 100hp when aboveground anyway. Deals 35 dmg and plays loud sound cue on impact. Costs 8 points

text:
Deadzoning: Switching between movement keys (A and D) continuously and shooting a bullet in the perfect accuracy window just after you switch to the opposite movement key.

Strafing: Hold one movement key (say A). Then release A [and tap D*] and shoot 1 or more bullets. Do the opposite now and repeat continuously. (*Tap part can be left out in Valorant as it is insignificant).

In deadzoning you are always moving. Whereas in strafing you come to a standstill for sometime.

My aim is to become a harder target to be hit in duels. Deadzoning will do that better, right?

Then why do some people and guides recommend strafing rather than deadzoning, or is it in some other context or situations other than being harder to hit (like in strafing you can burst fire)?
label: insight

text:
There's been a lot of talk about how Sage could possibly be reworked fitting her more to a role of "Battle Sage". And it got me thinking, how would Agent variations change the game? Like some Agents having other versions of themselves that slightly alter how they play.

What I noticed about Valorant is about how Agent never really changes. It's not like League where you can have different builds and runes every game, sometimes altering your champion's gameplay a lot. Valorant agents are just kinda static, not saying it as a bad thing though! The only system that seems to alter the Agent itself is the map. Then again, there is also the skill ceiling of the agent itself.

But what if there was another system. What if Agents had tiny ability variations that alters how they play? Like Sage's healing could be changed to minimal healing but with an added damage boost. Or being able to play Harbor's old kit back.

To be honest, I'm not super on board with this idea but it gives depth and new experiences to Agents we already know and love instead of frequently adding new members to the cast which ends up being forgettable sometimes.

Simply hypothetical but I wanna know what you guys think.
label: insight
